notes: proposed standard name for plant functional type

description: Proportion of daily carbon gain lost to growth respiration
notes: growth respiration factor is the fraction of new carbon gain (GPP - Rm) used in the growth of new tissues. This is an ED - specific parameter. In ED, Rg = growth_resp_factor * (GPP - Rm); where Rm is maintenance Respiration.
name: growth_resp_factor

notes: = rhizome + root, a.k.a. total belowground biomass. If rhizome biomass reported seperately, also enter this data seperately as a trait with variable name = rhizome_biomass

notes:
efficiency with which light is converted into fixed carbon.

mol CO2 fixed per mol irradiance. typical values ~ 0.06; not the same as ED parameter quantum_efficiency, which has same units but is ~ 0.08.

Clumping factor.  This factor indicates the degree of clumpiness of leaves in foliage canopies (i.e. omega parameter in LAI equation)

0 -- black hole
1 -- homogeneous, no clumping

For more info see: Kucharik et al., (1999), Chen et al., (2006) Ag For Met (doi:10.1016/j.agrformet.2008.01.020)

the flux of H2O out of the leaf, assumed to be the same as the flux of CO2 out of the leaf

DUPLICATE.  TO BE REMOVED

Covariate: min dbh  (DBH of smallest trees included in estimate.  = 0 when all plants are included)
name: Biomass_structural

description: The activation energy for Vcmax (Ev) derived from an Arrhenius model
notes: The activation energy for Vcmax (Ev) derived from an Arrhenius model.  This parameter is used to scale the value of Vcmax at the measurement temperature to an estimated value at another temperature based on the exponential form of the Arrhenius model:  f(Tk) = Vcmax25*exp[(Ev*(Tk-298.15))/(298.15*R*Tk)]; where Tk is the leaf temperature in Kelvin, Vcmax25 is the value of Vcmax at 25 degC, and R is the ideal gas constant.  When entered into BETY the Vcmax value at 25 degC should be entered as a covariate.  E.g. Vcmax25: 97.76, Ev: 54.08.
name: Ev_Arrhenius

notes: covariates: depth, root_diameter_min, root_diameter_max

notes: VPD = saturation vapor pressure (E_s) - vapor pressure (E_a)

description: Includes enlarged stems, roots, and tubers; NOT rhizomes; see notes
notes:
Tubers are enlarged roots, (in contrast to rhizomes, which are enlarged stems). 

This variable refers to three types of belowground storage organs:
* Tuberous Stems: Potatoes (belowground, but technically stems)
* Tuberous Roots: Cassava, Sweet Potato, Daylilly
* Root Crops: Beet, Carrot

This variable does not refer to rhizomes:
* Miscanthus, Strawberry

description: amount of CO2 released due to growth per unit net photosynthesis
notes:
This parameter is used in WIMOVAC and BioCro; in these models, Rg = g_R * Pn

This is distinct from the term g_R from Giffords 2003 eq 3 (equivalent to Amthor 2000 eq 2, where the term is expressed as mol C/g biomass):

R = g_R*G + m_R * W 

where G is the growth rate (new biomass s-1) and W is living biomass, because G = Pn - Rm - Rg

description: The activation energy for Jmax (Ej) derived from an Arrhenius model
notes: The activation energy for Jmax (Ej) derived from an Arrhenius model. This parameter is used to scale the value of Jmax at the measurement temperature to an estimated value at another temperature based on the exponential form of the Arrhenius model: f(Tk) = Jmax25*exp[(Ej*(Tk-298.15))/(298.15*R*Tk)]; where Tk is the leaf temperature in Kelvin, Jmax25 is the value of Jmax at 25 degC, and R is the ideal gas constant. When entered into BETY the Jmax value at 25 degC should be entered as a covariate.
name: Ej_Arrhenius

description: The parameter that describes the curvature (smoothness) between light-limited and CO2 limited photosynthetic rate
notes: The parameter that describes the curvature (smoothness) between light-limited and CO2 limited photosynthetic rate.  Also known as the "convexity" of the light response.  Originally described in Farqhuar & Wong (1984), An Empirical Model of Stomatal Conductance in equation A3 where they set convexity to 0.67 and then later described by Long and Hällgren 1993 and Bernacchi et al, 2003 in equations 2 and 7.   Unitless
